_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: DOM & Events practice 1
questions.prompt: What can `querySelector` return when no element matches?
choices: null
An empty string
false only
A new element
questions.explanation: The method returns null when it cannot find a matching element, so code should check before accessing properties.

questions.title: DOM & Events practice 2
questions.prompt: When does a click listener run?
choices: After the registered element receives a click
As soon as it is registered
Before the page loads every time
Only when the function returns text
questions.explanation: Registration stores the callback for later; the browser invokes it when the matching click event occurs.

questions.title: Trace DOM & Events
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
accepted: The displayed number increases after each click.
questions.explanation: The setup registers one listener. Each click then updates the stored count and the page text. The resulting output is 'The displayed number increases after each click.'.

questions.title: DOM & Events good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for DOM & Events?
choices: Select the intended element precisely.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Select the intended element precisely. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: DOM & Events practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices:
const target = document.querySelector("#missing");
if (target) target.textContent = "Ready";
document.querySelector("#missing").textContent = "Ready";
Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: Selection can fail when the id is wrong or the script runs before the element exists. Check the returned value before using it.

questions.title: DOM & Events practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Use textContent for plain text'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
`innerHTML` asks the browser to parse markup. Use `textContent` for ordinary user-facing text, especially when a value came from input, so text is displayed rather than treated as page structure.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: `innerHTML` asks the browser to parse markup. Use `textContent` for ordinary user-facing text, especially when a value came from input, so text is displayed rather than treated as page structure.

questions.title: DOM & Events practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for DOM & Events?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Check a selector result before use.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Check a selector result before use. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: DOM & Events practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for DOM & Events?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Keep listeners short and focused.
questions.explanation: Keep listeners short and focused. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: DOM & Events practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for DOM & Events?
choices: Display untrusted text with textContent.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Display untrusted text with textContent. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: DOM & Events practice 10
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
accepted: The displayed number increases after each click.
questions.explanation: The setup registers one listener. Each click then updates the stored count and the page text. The output is 'The displayed number increases after each click.'.
